default.water: Moderate; consistent moisture — drought stress causes premature bolting
default.soil: Rich, well-drained loam · pH 6.5–7.5; slightly higher pH than most vegetables
default.frost: Very cold hardy — germinates in near-freezing soil; survives to 20°F; finest flavor after frost
tips: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring; sow again in late August for fall
Sow seeds ½ inch deep, 2 inches apart; thin to 4–6 inches for full-size leaves
Succession sow every 2 weeks through April for extended spring harvest
Spinach bolts quickly in heat and long days — choose bolt-resistant varieties for spring
Fall spinach is sweeter, slower to bolt, and more productive than spring; prioritize it
In Zone 8b: fall sowing in August produces through November and overwinters for spring
harvest: Harvest outer leaves once plants have 6+ leaves — always leave the growing center intact
Or cut the whole plant 1 inch above soil level — it may resprout for a second harvest
Morning harvest gives the sweetest, most tender leaves
At first sign of a flower stalk (bolting) — harvest entire plant immediately; flavor declines fast
companions: ✅ Strawberries — spinach fills space between rows; shade from strawberry leaves is welcome
✅ Peas — classic cool-season companions; peas fix nitrogen spinach appreciates
✅ Radishes — deter leaf miners; same sowing conditions and timing
✅ Garlic & onions — aromatic deterrents to aphids that target spinach
❌ Fennel — inhibits spinach growth when planted nearby
❌ Beets — same family (Chenopodiaceae); share diseases and some pests
pests: Leaf miners — white squiggly trails in leaves; remove affected leaves; row covers prevent flies
Aphids — especially in early spring; knock off with water; encourage ladybugs
Downy mildew — choose resistant varieties; avoid overhead watering in cool, humid weather
Slugs — especially on young seedlings; apply iron phosphate bait at soil level
default.safe: 🐱 🐶 Non-toxic to cats and dogs; raw spinach safe for humans in moderation

harvest: Deeply crinkled, dark green leaves — excellent texture for salads and sautéing
Harvest outer leaves at 45–50 days; cold-hardy through Zone 4
Crinkled surface traps grit — wash thoroughly before use
Bloomsdale Long Standing is the classic savoy; excellent bolt resistance for spring
Smooth, broad, dark green leaves — easier to wash than savoy types
Harvest outer leaves at 38–45 days; slightly less cold-hardy than savoy
Excellent for juicing, smoothies, and fresh salads
Preferred by commercial growers for ease of processing; great for home use too

default.sun: Full sun to partial shade; afternoon shade extends harvest season in warm weather
default.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant; consistent moisture slows bolting
default.soil: Well-drained, moderately fertile loam · pH 6.2–6.8
default.frost: Hardy to light frost; best sown in cool weather when bolting risk is lowest
tips: Direct sow in place — cilantro dislikes transplanting; sow seeds ¼ inch deep, 6 inches apart
Succession sow every 2–3 weeks March through May, then again in late August
Cilantro bolts very quickly in heat and long days — most spring sowings will run to seed by June
Let some plants go to seed (coriander) — seeds are a spice, and plants self-sow prolifically
Fall sowings in late August are often the most productive; plants overwinter in Zone 8b
Crush a leaf to test flavor — some people have a genetic sensitivity (tastes like soap)
harvest: Harvest outer leaves once plants have 4+ true leaves — young leaves have the best flavor
Harvest in the morning before volatile oils disperse in the afternoon heat
At first sign of a flower stalk — harvest the feathery top leaves; they're more delicate and flavorful
Let some plants flower for coriander seed harvest; collect seeds when tan-brown and before they drop
companions: ✅ Spinach & lettuce — cool-season companions that share sowing conditions
✅ Tomatoes & peppers — flowers attract beneficial insects that prey on tomato pests
✅ Beans — cilantro flowers attract predatory wasps that control bean aphids
✅ Anise — said to improve cilantro germination when planted nearby
❌ Fennel — compete for space; fennel's allelopathic properties can affect cilantro
❌ Lavender — conflicting moisture needs; cilantro prefers more regular watering
pests: Aphids — on new growth and flower heads; knock off with water; minimal pest pressure
Whiteflies — occasional; yellow sticky traps; insecticidal soap if severe
Cilantro is generally low-maintenance; most issues come from bolting, not pests
default.safe: 🐱 🐶 Non-toxic to cats and dogs (ASPCA)

default.sun: Full sun (6–8 hrs/day); tolerates light shade
default.water: Moderate; consistent moisture — dry soil causes tough, stringy stems
default.soil: Well-drained, fertile loam · pH 6.0–7.0; loose soil for best stem development
default.frost: Hardy to light frost; spring and fall sowings work well in Zone 8b
tips: Direct sow ½ inch deep, 1 inch apart, rows 12 inches; thin to 2 inches for full-size scallions
Succession sow every 2–3 weeks from March through May and again Aug–Sept for year-round supply
Can also regrow from kitchen scraps — save root ends in a glass of water or plant directly
Harvest as scallions (pencil-thin, 6–8 inches tall) or leave to develop fuller bunching onions
In Zone 8b: fall sowings overwinter and are among the first harvests of spring
harvest: Harvest when stems are pencil-thick and 6–8 inches tall — typically 60–80 days from sowing
Cut at soil level with scissors; the plant will often resprout for a second cut
Or pull entire clump; separate and use immediately for freshest flavor
Refrigerate in a glass of water for up to 1 week; tops stay crisp and fresh
companions: ✅ Carrots — aromatic scent deters carrot fly; excellent interplant
✅ Lettuce & radishes — share cool-season timing; fill space between rows
✅ Tomatoes — deterrent to aphids and spider mites at base of plants
✅ Brassicas — deter aphids and imported cabbageworm
❌ Peas & beans — allelopathic; alliums inhibit legume root nodule development
❌ Fennel — inhibits most vegetables including alliums
pests: Onion thrips — yellow or silver streaking on leaves; spray with water or insecticidal soap
Onion fly — less common on fast-growing scallion types than bulbing onions
Downy mildew — avoid overhead watering; improve airflow by correct spacing
Aphids — occasional; knock off with water spray
default.safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats and dogs — all alliums contain thiosulphate; keep pets away

harvest: The classic bunching onion — harvest as soon as stems reach pencil thickness at 6–8 inches
Evergreen Bunching is the standard variety — mild flavor, excellent cold tolerance
Harvest whole plant or cut at soil level for regrowth; multiple cuts per sowing possible
Excellent raw as garnish, in Asian dishes, salads, and as a quick flavor base

default.sun: Full sun to partial shade; 4–6 hrs minimum for good growth
default.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established; avoid waterlogging
default.soil: Well-drained, moderately fertile loam · pH 6.0–7.0; tolerates poor soil better than most herbs
default.frost: Very cold hardy perennial — dies back in winter but re-emerges reliably each spring
tips: Direct sow or transplant in early spring; chives are slow from seed — starting from a division or plug is faster
Snip leaves with scissors to ½ inch above soil — the plant will regrow vigorously
Divide clumps every 3 years to maintain vigor and prevent overcrowding
Let some flowers develop — purple blooms are edible, attractive to pollinators, and stunning
In Zone 8b: clumps re-emerge in late February and can be harvested March through December
Chive flowers make beautiful garnishes and can be added to herb vinegars
harvest: Harvest by cutting stems to ½–1 inch above the soil — new growth follows in 2–3 weeks
Morning harvest gives the most intense flavor and best shelf life
Use fresh — chives lose much of their flavor when cooked; add at the very end
Flowers are edible and have a milder onion flavor than the leaves
Refrigerate wrapped in damp paper towel up to 1 week; or freeze chopped in olive oil
companions: ✅ Carrots — chive scent deters carrot fly; excellent row companion
✅ Tomatoes — repels aphids; improves flavor when planted nearby
✅ Roses — classic companion; reduces aphid pressure and fungal diseases
✅ Brassicas — aromatic deterrent to cabbage moth and aphids
❌ Peas & beans — allelopathic; chives (allium) inhibit legume growth
❌ Fennel — allelopathic; inhibits chives and most neighboring plants
pests: Onion thrips — occasional; spray with water or insecticidal soap if found
Aphids — rare on chives; the aromatic oils are a natural repellent
Rust (orange pustules on leaves) — improve airflow; avoid overhead watering
Chives are generally one of the most pest-free herbs in the garden
default.safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats and dogs — all alliums contain thiosulphate; keep pets away

default.sun: Full sun (6 hrs minimum); afternoon shade acceptable in hot summers
default.water: Moderate; deep watering weekly once established; reduce in fall
default.soil: Well-drained, fertile loam · pH 6.0–7.0; amend with compost and bone meal at planting
default.frost: Hardy to Zone 3; cold winters actually improve bloom performance — need vernalization
tips: Plant bare roots in fall (Oct–Nov) with eyes no more than 1–2 inches below soil surface — too deep = no blooms
Peonies are permanent — choose their location carefully; they thrive undisturbed for 30+ years
Do not cut back foliage until after frost — leaves feed the tuber through fall for next year's blooms
Stake or use peony rings before blooms open — heavy flowers flop in rain without support
In Zone 8b: peonies bloom May–June; they need the cold winters to vernalize and set flower buds
Young plants bloom lightly in years 1–2; full spectacular bloom begins in year 3
harvest: Cut blooms when buds are soft and showing color but petals are still closed (marshmallow stage)
Harvest in the morning into cool water; immediately refrigerate to extend vase life
Sear cut ends briefly in boiling water or over flame to extend vase life to 7–10 days
Peonies can be stored as tight buds in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks — perfect for events
companions: ✅ Alliums (decorative onion) — bloom simultaneously; beautiful color contrast
✅ Salvia — shares well-drained soil preference; extends the bloom season
✅ Roses — classic cottage garden pairing; similar care requirements
✅ Lady's Mantle — low-growing edging plant that hides dying peony foliage
❌ Tulips & bulbs — competing root zones when planted too close; give peonies 3 feet clearance
pests: Botrytis blight (gray mold) — most common; wilting black stems in spring; remove affected parts; improve airflow
Ants on buds — they harvest nectar and do no harm; do not spray
Phytophthora blight — soggy soil causes crown rot; ensure excellent drainage
Thrips on flowers — cause streaking on petals; use insecticidal soap if severe
default.safe: ⚠️ Mildly toxic to cats and dogs — can cause vomiting and diarrhea if ingested (ASPCA)

default.sun: Full sun (6+ hrs/day); spring sun is critical before canopy trees leaf out
default.water: Low; bulbs rot in wet soil — good drainage is non-negotiable
default.soil: Well-drained, sandy or loamy soil · pH 6.0–7.0; never plant in clay without amendment
default.frost: Fully hardy bulbs; need cold winter vernalization to bloom; plant in fall
tips: Plant bulbs 6–8 inches deep, pointed end up, in October–November in Zone 8b
Add sharp sand or grit to planting holes if your soil drains slowly
In mild Zone 8b winters, tulips may need pre-chilling: store bulbs in refrigerator (not freezer) for 6 weeks before planting
Let foliage die back naturally after bloom — do not cut green leaves; they recharge the bulb
In Pacific Northwest wet winters, consider lifting and storing bulbs after foliage dies back for better repeat bloom
Treat as annuals for guaranteed performance — replant fresh bulbs each fall for the showiest display
harvest: Cut stems at an angle when petals are just beginning to show color but before fully open
Immediately place in cool water in a dark room for 24 hours — they condition beautifully
Tulips continue to grow in the vase — cut stems ½ inch daily to maintain water uptake
Vase life 5–10 days; keep away from direct sunlight and fruit (ethylene gas shortens life)
companions: ✅ Forget-me-nots — classic cottage pairing; blue flowers perfectly complement tulip colors
✅ Pansies — overlap in spring bloom time; fill space between tulip stems
✅ Alliums — bloom just after tulips fade; extend the seasonal display seamlessly
✅ Hostas — emerge as tulip foliage fades, hiding the dying leaves naturally
❌ Tulip bulbs near shallow-rooted annuals — don't disturb roots when planting summer annuals
pests: Squirrels and voles — bury wire mesh baskets around bulbs; plant with daffodils (toxic to rodents) as deterrent
Tulip fire (Botrytis) — most serious disease; remove and destroy affected plants; don't replant in same spot
Aphids — remove with water spray; less serious on tulips than on vegetables
Slugs on emerging foliage in spring — apply iron phosphate bait at soil level
default.safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats and dogs — bulbs and flowers can cause drooling, vomiting, and cardiac issues (ASPCA)

default.sun: Full sun (6+ hrs/day); cool morning sun ideal — afternoon shade appreciated
default.water: Moderate; consistent moisture during growth; reduce sharply after flowers fade
default.soil: Well-drained, fertile, light loam · pH 6.0–7.0; heavy clay causes corm rot
default.frost: Semi-hardy corms; Zone 8b can plant in fall (October) or late winter (February)
tips: Pre-soak corms (octopus-shaped, tentacles down) in cool water for 4 hours before planting
Plant corms 2 inches deep, claws-down, 6 inches apart in fall (Oct) or late winter (Feb–Mar)
Fall planting in Zone 8b overwinters under light mulch and blooms March–May
Late winter planting blooms April–June — slightly later but avoids any cold-snap risk
After bloom and foliage yellows, lift and dry corms in a shaded, airy spot; store cool and dry until fall
In Zone 8b, corms can also stay in well-drained ground with light mulch in mild winters
harvest: Cut stems when buds are just beginning to open — they continue to open beautifully in the vase
Harvest in the morning into cool water; ranunculus are among the finest cut flowers
Vase life 7–10 days; recut stems every 2 days and change water to maximize longevity
Multi-petaled blooms resemble roses — layers of tissue-thin petals open over several days
companions: ✅ Anemones — bloom simultaneously in spring; excellent color companions in a cutting garden
✅ Sweet peas — share cool-season bloom; beautiful for mixed spring arrangements
✅ Tulips — similar fall planting time; complementary heights and colors in spring
✅ Pansies — fill space between emerging ranunculus stems in early spring
pests: Powdery mildew — most common issue late in season; improve airflow; water at base only
Aphids on stems and buds — spray with water; follow with insecticidal soap if severe
Slugs on emerging shoots — apply iron phosphate bait at soil level in early spring
Crown rot — almost always caused by overwatering or poorly drained soil; prioritize drainage
default.safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats, dogs, and horses — all parts contain ranunculin; keep pets away (ASPCA)
